desc:
The road continues to climbs steadily from the plains below, up towards the
mountain spur that cuts across the land just south of here. Turning from the
north to the east, it makes its way relentlessly toward the mouth of a narrow
pass that can be seen in the distance. The smooth red stones that paved the
surface of this road have become pitted and cracked in the long years since they
were laid down, and now this is little more than a mountain trail.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
The road sweeps back and forth across the land making several massive
switchbacks. They climb from the plains below up to the altitude of a pass
through the mountain spur that looms not far south of here. The red stone that
was laid so long ago to provide a smooth surface for the road is now cracked and
broken.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
This section of the road is largely intact, with most of its red stone surface
free of any significant pits or cracks. Travelling in large arcs, the road
snakes back and forth, following the contours of the land to gain the altitude
it needs for its journey between the lower-lying plains and a mountain pass that
lies to the southeast.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
The road twists and turns, snaking across the land from the low-lying plains, up
towards a mountain pass that lies to the southeast of here. Though the road is
in a poor state of repair, its red paving stones are largely intact, with only a
few hardy weeds and shrubs making a brave attempt to encroach upon them. Here
and there a thick, thorny bush sprouts up beside the road.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
Broken red stone litters the area, barely discernible under the large amount of
grasses and silt that have settled here. The eastern and northern edges of the
road are completely overgrown with thick thorny brush that seems to never end.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
The road turns here, picking up and following to the east the highest ground, so
that it can make a steady ascent toward the mouth of a mountain pass visible a
couple of leagues or so to the southeast. To the north it seems instead to seek
the lower, more sheltered parts of the plains, though meagre is the protection
offered from the elements in any part of this land. Large ruts and cracks
bedevil the road. Where once there was a smooth surface of red stone to pave the
way, now only shattered fragments remain, with grasses and weeds slowly taking
over.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
The road starts an almost imperceptible ascent, as it travels from north to
south across the plains, drawing slowly closer to a distant mountain spur. Here
and there, it almost vanishes from sight, the flat red stones that once formed
its smooth surface having become cracked and overgrown with weeds and coarse,
yellow-brown grasses. Also, deposits of soft, silty soil further cover the
paving.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
Little remains of what was a road, thinning to little more than a footpath here
with silt and sand covering the red stone. Small bushes have grown over the road
while grass covers the rest of the surrounding area. There is little evidence of
any traffic that might utilise this path. The trail continues to the west and
widens to the south.
name: Remains of a Road

desc:
Thick brush from fields to the north and south encroach on this part of the
road, making it nearly impassable. The red stones that once paved the route are
now cracked and covered by soil and dirt. What remains of the surface is now
little better than a rough trail. The road widens slightly, and its condition
improves markedly further to the west, while it narrows more to the east.
name: Barely Discernible Road

desc:
Many small rocks have been uprooted here, and have all but turned the road into
a stony field. As if to help the transformation, weeds of all types have grown
up between the rocks. To the south, a field grows out of the cobblestones, while
what is left of the road continues to the north and east. To the west a large
rock formation blocks the way, making it impossible to continue in that
direction.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
The road continues to the north and south of here, meeting the brush to the east
without clear transition as the road is overgrown with weeds and grasses of all
types. The red chalky stone that originally composed this pavement is full of
small juts and large cracks.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
The road here is overgrown with weeds and grasses, and large boulders jut out of
what was once smooth paving. Cracks are evident everywhere in the rocks that
were used in the road. A thick patch of brush grows to the north, clinging to
the meagre shelter offered by a small plateau. The road continues to the east
and south.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
Large rocks have been uprooted in the road here, making what was once smooth so
long ago, a bumpy and unsteady road. The rocks themselves are worn with time and
weather. There is only a slight transition between brush and broken road to the
south and east, while the road continues to the west and north.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
This narrow road appears to have fallen into disrepair from lack of use. The
surface of the road is rough and grass has begun to creep over the sides of the
road from the surrounding fields. A tableland lies to the west, and to the east
there are grassy hills.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
The road makes a sharp bend at this point to avoid the tributary that runs
directly to the north and east. Judging by the poor condition of the pavement,
it would appear this roadway is no longer maintained. The banks of the river are
too steep to descend safely. The road continues to the west and south.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
This ancient road makes its way through the lands, twisting and turning around
the tablelands and streams. The ford to the north allows easy passage across a
tributary, and to the south a short slope rises to a tableland. Far into the
distance to the southeast, the outline of the Misty Mountains is clearly defined
against the horizon.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
Broad and level, the tableland rises higher than the surrounding lands. A soft
grass thickly covers the top of the plateau and a few wildflowers can be found
growing in the grass, raising their heads towards the sun. A faint road can be
seen twisting its way through the plains, and beyond that green fields extend
out next to a meandering stream.
name: Tableland

desc:
Flowing from east to northwest, the water level drops sufficiently to allow one
to cross the tributary safely and easily. The height and depth of the bank
indicates that the level of water was once much deeper at this point and the
river bed itself is cluttered with various rocks and debris that became too
large or heavy to be carried along with the current. The rough remains of an
ancient roadway continue on to the south and north, winding their way through
the plains.
name: Shallow Ford

desc:
The tributary is shallow enough south of here to be easily crossed on foot or
riding. The flow of the water is soft and smooth, swirling against the edges of
the river bank and sending the occasional cloud of sand and dirt into the water.
To the north, grassy plains slope upwards towards a plateau. The rough remains
of this ancient road become more visible to the east.
name: North of the Ford

desc:
Bending past the large body of water flowing to the south, this small roadway
heads to the north and west. A low point in the stream to the southwest offers
an excellent place to cross the wide tributary and reach the lands beyond. Upon
a small hill to the north stands some old ruins and tents that overlook the
plains and this ancient, crumbling thoroughfare.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
The old and crumbling road runs along the field the north, where a camp has been
built by the ruins. Continuing east and south, the road continues on its way
through these barren lands. From the appearance of the roadway, it would seem
this area is seldom frequented. The surface of the roadway is uneven and strewn
with small rocks and stones. A grassy incline rises gently to the west.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
The small stream visible to the south forces the road to make a lazy turn and
continue both north and west. It would be too difficult to cross the stream at
this point without getting wet due to its steep banks and high water line, but
it appears possible to ford the stream farther to the southwest.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
The road runs straight by a fenced camp to the west, while to the north a
landscape with lots of brushes is visible. Judging by the dilapidated condition
of the road, it sees little traffic, and is maintained even less. On the other
hand, the camp to the west seems to be in good repair, suggesting that perhaps
the area is not completely deserted.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
The road continues straight on through the plains, heading from east to west.
Short shrubs sprout up along an earthen bank to the north, their tangled and
thorny branches making passage difficult. Beneath the shrubs, a vertical drop
leads down to a depression, but it is too steep to climb. A small meadow lies to
the south.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
A sharp bend turns the direction of the road to the west and north. The road is
in poor condition with several bumps and rocks from lack of maintenance but is
still an easier route to choose than travelling straight through the plains. In
the south, a small stream flows happily by. To the east the, the plains seem to
stretch out for many miles. Beyond that, some hills are visible.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
Stretching out north and south, the road heads on through the grassy plains. The
upright drop to the west would make travel in that direction impossible, but
entering the brown fields, that border the road to the east, is quite possible.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
Turning to avoid a depression in a field to the north, the road continues to the
east and south. It is covered with potholes and broken stones, and the bushes
growing on the bank to the west are beginning to spread out and creep over the
roadway. Beyond the bushes to the west, the ground suddenly disappears exposing
a depression in a field.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
Twisting and turning through the fields and plains, the road makes a wide arc
and continues on to the north and west. There are small rocks and stones on the
ground and a narrow ditch choked with weeds and grass borders the roadway on
either side.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
This ancient road turns to bend around a depression to the west, continuing to
the east and south. The surface of the road is cracked and crumbled in places,
and the grass from the plains is beginning to creep onto the road as it lies
unused in this desolate area. A thorny thicket blocks the way to the north.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
The narrow road winds its way through the countryside, bending to travel west
and north. Apparently, this road is no longer used frequently as nature is
beginning to reclaim the ground as its own. The roadway is bordered on both
sides by a sea of tall weeds and there is a strip of grass straight down the
middle of the road.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
Lack of use and care has caused the condition of this narrow road to degenerate
but there is still a well-marked pathway visible through the plains that travels
north and south in direction. Thorny thickets prevent the travel to the west. To
the east, a ridge of loose gravel ascends too steeply to climb.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
The wide, once well-travelled road finds its way from the east to the south. A
smaller, apparently less travelled trail leads westward away from the road and
through the surrounding fields. A faint rock wall can be seen further to the
west.
name: Fork in the Road

desc:
This large, dusty road, running from the east to the west, is paved with badly
worn bricks and stones reduced to mere pebbles, to allow large wagons to travel
upon it. The road seems to have been well-used once upon a time. Dry, barren
plains lie in all directions.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
This wide road finds its way from north to west. Paved with stones and bricks,
travel by large and heavy wagons would still be possible despite the obvious
lack of maintenance. The road runs through sloping, barren fields that stretch
out as far as the eye can see, save for the rock wall cliff that looms over the
road to the northwest. The only forms of vegetation nearby are small patches of
dry grass or the occasional thorn bush or small, misshapen tree.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
The ancient, broken road slowly curves its way from the north to the south. The
road is paved with large bricks and broken stones, allowing large wagons to
travel upon it. A steep, rock wall looms over the road to the north and the
west. The road has been built slightly higher than the surrounding plains to the
southeast, allowing excessive rainwater to flow away. The steep cliffs to the
north and west do not allow passage.
name: Ancient Broken Road

desc:
This hill rises toward the southeast, ending in a sudden cliff. The cliff cannot
be climbed. The ground on this hilltop is filled with dry, brown grass and
rarely some thorny bushes. Small rocks and broken boulders lie scattered on the
hard ground, which is a mixture of clay and limestone. A road can be spotted to
the southeast.
name: Jutting Hill

desc:
This dry, brown hill juts from the southeast toward the northwest, lowering into
flat fields north and west. Most of these barren wastelands are covered with
patches of dark brown, dry, grass. The ground, consisting of what seems to be a
mixture of clay and limestone, is too hard and dry to allow ploughing or any
profitable agriculture.
name: Brown Hill

desc:
Reaching out to the horizon in all directions, these sloping plains are covered
with small patches of dry, brown grass, interspersed with the occasional small,
thorny bush. The soil below is clay-like and infertile. Small rocks and broken
boulders are scattered around the plains. The eastward slope of the plains rises
gradually to a hilltop. The occasional high-pitched cry of a bird can be heard
in the distance.
name: Dried-out Plains

desc:
These barren plains, vegetated mostly with dark patches of dry, brown grass, lie
directly to the north of a small trail, that runs from west to south. The fields
are covered with broken stones and small boulders, whilst every here and there,
a small thorny shrub or lone tree sprouts up.
name: Dry Grasslands

desc:
This dry, barren hill is covered with brown patches of grass, interspersed with
some small thorn bushes and a few lone trees. The ground drops to level fields
to the west and the hill continues to the north, coming to another hill. The way
east and south is blocked by a cliff too steep to climb down. The cliff
overlooks a wide road.
name: Dry Hill above the Road

desc:
The only thing that distinguishes this area from the lands that surround it is
that the thorny bushes grow a little less far apart here than they do in the
more barren parts of the plain. They stand in small thickets scattered amongst
the patches of wasted brown grass. Dry and brittle, the bushes are horribly
misshapen.
name: Fields of Scattered Brush

desc:
A vast plain of dry, brown grass and a few thorny bushes stretches in all
directions. A massive, impassable ridge can be seen running from the north to
the southwest. The trail here runs from the west and turns south.
name: Small Trail through the Fields

desc:
A small, dusty path, paved with bricks and small stones, connects to a larger
road a little way to the east. The trail leads to the north, into the fields.
The surrounding area is barren, covered only with dry patches of grass and some
lone thorny bushes. The high-pitched cry of a bird can be heard from time to
time.
name: Small Dusty Trail

desc:
Barren plains, covered with patches of dry grass, stretch out as far as the eye
can see. The only other form of vegetation present is small, misshapen, thorny
bushes. The clay and limestone ground is quite hard, rendering any form of
agriculture near impossible. The ground is covered with debris. A small trail
can be spotted to the north.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
The barren plains stretch out in all directions save the north and west, where a
sharp ridge blocks the view. Small rocks cover the hard ground, which seems to
be a mixture of clay and limestone. The plains are vegetated with small patches
of brown, dry grass and every here and there is a scarce thicket of thorn
bushes. A small trail lies to the north.
name: Plains near a Ridge

desc:
The trail continues to the west towards an abandoned farm house whilst to the
southeast, the trail makes its way towards a larger road. The plains are barren
and scarcely vegetated, with only some small thorny bushes and a lone tree to be
seen between the dry patches of grass.
name: Small Trail through the Fields

desc:
A small trail, paved with small bricks and stones, continues to the north
towards a distant cabin to the northwest. Eastwards, the trail leads towards
larger road. On all sides the trail is surrounded by sloping hills, whose sole
vegetation consists of a few lone, misshapen trees and a carpet of dry, brown
grass. No flowers can be spotted anywhere, unable to survive in this barren
landscape.
name: Small Trail over a Hill

desc:
This small trail runs from the west to the south. The trail is paved with
several stones which prevent it becoming a pool of mud during heavy rain. The
trail winds through the sloping hills that run along a ridgeline. The grass that
grows on the sides of the path is brown and dry.
name: Small Trail

desc:
The sloping fields are largely barren, vegetated only by scattered patches of
dry, brown grass and a few small, thorny bushes. A small trail marches past to
the east and north, leading travellers between a larger road that lies to the
southeast, and the ruins of a farm house alongside a rickety cabin. Small rocks
and broken stones are scattered throughout the area.
name: Sloping Hills near a Trail

desc:
The brown and barren grasses spread out across this rolling hill. They are
vegetated by extremely thick, misshapen thorn bushes and small patches of dark
brown, dried-out grass, whilst some lone trees grow in a few places. Mossy
stones lie scattered here and there. The ridge line that runs from the northeast
to the southwest is characterized by the steep cliff that runs along the
southeast.
name: Barren Hill

desc:
Consisting mainly of dry grass, weeds and sparse bushes, these grasslands
stretch out as far as the eye can see. A few violet flowers grow in a small
circle, the only flora that managed to bloom here. There are hills to the south
and east that are covered in brown, dried grass.
name: Sloping Grassland

desc:
These grassy hills continue to undulate to the east and south. The ground on
this hilltop is characterized by small, brown patches of grass. Small clumps of
bushes grow scattered amongst the hilltop grasses, whilst a lone holly-tree
stands near the top of this small hill which rises above the sloping fields to
the north and west.
name: Dry Grassy Hilltop

desc:
These desert-like plains are dry and barren. Patches of brown grass sparsely
cover the hard ground, which appears to be composed of a clay and limestone
mixture. The occasional lone tree or thorn shrub valiantly rises into the air,
but no other flora is visible. Nothing suggests the fauna would be richer than
the flora.
name: Barren Wasteland

desc:
The slopes are covered with brown patches of grass and dried-out vegetation,
interspersed with the occasional, misshapen tree. A pool of mud can be seen to
the north. The grasslands seem to stretch out to the horizon in all directions.
Sometimes the cry of a bird of prey can be heard in the distance.
name: Sloping Grassland

desc:
A small source of water starts here, slowly flowing to the north. A thick muddy
pool has formed close by, some of the mud following the stream. Small patches of
coarse grass grow near the pool, while the bones of small animals that once
became stuck, stick out of the mud. The odour of rotten flesh fills the area.
Endless, sloping plains lie to the south.
name: Muddy Pool at the Start of a Stream

desc:
Dry weeds grow amongst the ancient cobblestones that line this floor. A
crumbling stone wall remains as a reminder of where the old walls stood where
this ruined farm house once stood. A shabby looking cabin has been constructed
to the east reusing a portion of the foundations of the old farmhouse.
name: Ruined Farmhouse

desc:
The small trail stops to the east where an ancient, crumbling rock wall seems
indicate the ruins of a farm house property. A rather shabby-looking cabin has
been constructed more recently on the foundations of the ruins. Seemingly, empty
plains lie to both the south and north, and continue into the horizon.
name: End of a Small Trail

desc:
Dry, brown patches of grass cover almost the entire landscape. Where the grass
is absent, the ground underfoot is composed of hard-packed, red-brown clay.
Small stones and broken rocks lie scattered throughout the area. A small trail
lies to the south and to the west, providing easy passage for travellers between
an abandoned farm house to the west and a road that can be seen far to the east.
A lone tree stands in the fields.
name: Dry Hills

desc:
The ridge stretches in north-east direction many miles, and to the south-west.
Standing at an edge, it's windy here. Visibility is clear here, no trees or many
shrubs to break the skyline and the only sign of any vegetation is a thick
blanket of grass that covers the ground. The steep ridge descends quickly to the
east and south.
name: Edge of a Ridge

desc:
Poor soil quality and the lack of a constant water source provide little growth
opportunity for almost all vegetation in this region of the hills. The ground is
cracked in several places and sparse patches of a short, brown grass crop up
occasionally, clinging to life despite the adverse conditions. The hilly plains
stretch out in all directions, as far as the eye can see.
name: Dry Hills

desc:
The hard dirt is only occasionally interrupted by a small tuft of bunch grass.
The hills to the north are dry and dusty. To the east the ridge descends
abruptly. To the south, bushes and shrubs can be seen sprouting out from the
grassland. The only noticeable sound is the soft rustle of the grass as the wind
blows across the hills.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
Various dips and short inclines in these hills make travel difficult and limit
the view in all directions. The occasional straggly bush or shrub can be seen
sprouting up, giving a marginal amount of cover for the small animals that
inhabit the area, and the grass grows short and thin in the depleted soil.
name: Uneven Hills

desc:
The land falls away to the east, creating a sandy ridge here, that levels out
into a plateau to the south. A fairly easy descent to the east leads directly
into a thicket of thorn bushes. To the north a vast expanse of hills is visible,
stretching out as far as the eye can see. Small tufts of grass cling to the
ridge with a stubborn resiliency, undaunted by the constant exposure to the wind
and sun at this elevated level.
name: Uneven Grounds

desc:
Around the old ruins to the east, there has been set up several tents. Someone
has apparently made the ruins a temporary home for themselves. The short grass
is crushed and trampled by constant use. Around the tents, a sturdy fence has
been built from wooden planks and stones. Some smoke rises up from the centre of
the camp area. To the south, one can see beyond the plains to the ford that
crosses a small tributary, and a jagged ridge juts out to the north.
name: Raised Plateau

desc:
The plains gradually begin to slope upwards towards a plateau visible in the
distance to the north. The lay of the land has enough of a slant that one can
imagine the plains towards the river to the south can become extremely soggy
during the rainy season, as the water runs down the subtle incline. The short
grass grows lush and thick in that section of the land but begins to thin out as
the land climbs. The outline of a road is discernible to the east and south.
name: Ascending Plains

desc:
The plains are even throughout, growing with a thick bed of grass approximately
a foot in height. The grass is a thick green carpet, capable of hiding small
animals. Various tracks and trails can be seen where deer and other creatures
have waded through. To the north the grass fields continue while to the west the
fields dip and become uneven.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
This is the end of the long ridge that stretches to the north. The slope to the
west is consisting mostly of loose gravel and soft sand, and that makes climbing
up the slope impossible. Some grass sticks out on the top of the ridge. As the
wind ripples through the fields of grass, the effect that it leaves is one of an
ocean's rolling waves.
name: Under a Steep Ridge

desc:
This is a strip of fields between the ridge to the west and a depression to the
south and east. Nothing else marks this barren landscape in any way. The grass
is slightly shorter in the west, enough perhaps to afford cover to small
animals, but little else.
name: Brink of a Depression

desc:
The grass field stretches for miles to the east, while a steep ridge resides to
the west. The scenery changes little to the south as grasses continue to thrive
unabated by stone or tree. The plains dry out considerably to the north and east
becoming little more than dirt and dried weeds. The ridge to the west and north
is impossible to climb because of steepness and loose gravel.
name: Under a Steep Ridge

desc:
The grass continues to grow with little change. No large tree or stone exists
here and the grass does not grow very high. Very little can be heard except for
the passing winds through the grass, which gives a ripple effect to the fields.
A sudden depression appears to have been formed to the south. Descend is very
steep, creating unclimbable rampart.
name: Precipice of a Depression

desc:
There is little to this area except the small grasses which subsist on the dry
soil. Nothing stirs, except the grass when the wind blows through it. A sharp
decline drops suddenly to the south, looking like the ground has caved in. The
fields continue to the east and west.
name: Precipice of a Depression

desc:
Small bunch grasses grow here with little to interrupt them. The winds blow
across the field so that it resembles waves upon an ocean. The fields continue
to the west with no interruption. There is a broken road that encircles this
field to the east and south, and a small dusty trail leads away from the field
to the north. However, thickets of thorny plants prevent travelling to the east
and south.
name: Flat Grasslands

desc:
This is bottom of a depression that goes on to the east and south. The slope is
very steep to the north and west. On the ground, there grows only little grass
and some thistles and brushes here and there. Otherwise the soil is dry and void
of life. A whirl of wind sometimes rises some dust to the air.
name: Bottom of a Depression

desc:
The ground elevates quickly to the north and east. Some kind of natural event
has formed this deep depression in the middle of vast fields of grass. The soil
in the bottom of the depression is quite dry, and have some cracks formed here
and there. There are markings of dried up water puddles on the ground. Some
yellowish grass seems to be sticking out of top of the brink.
name: Bottom of a Depression

desc:
In the middle of this depression, in the vast plains, there are not many
interesting features. The wall of the depression rises steeply to the east
giving no chance for climbing up to the plains. The ground here consists of
cracked, dried up mud. Depression continues to the north, south and west.
name: Middle of a Depression

desc:
Small, dying bushes hug the dried soil here in the bottom of a depression. Amid
the shrubs, several small boulders can be seen, embedded in the dry soil, and
even these are cracked, slowly disintegrating as a result of the wind and cold
winter frosts. More bushes can be seen to the west in the slope of depression.
name: Thorny Depression

desc:
This is the western end of the depression. The ground is cracked in many places
from the dryness and it is mostly devoid of plants. Only a few thistle and weed
grow here and there. The walls of the depression are too steep to climb and
there is no handhold visible. Upper, near to the edge, a few root protrudes out
of the dried up muddy wall.
name: Bottom of a Depression

desc:
The brush that springs up thick and wild in this portion of the land obstructs
but does not completely block travel through the plains. Several bird nests have
been built within the mesh of the tangled bushes. The partially concealed thorns
and twisted branches of the bushes provide some reliable source of protection
from snakes and other predators. There is a large thicket to the west and ground
drops quickly to the north. An overgrown road runs through the countryside south
of here.
name: Tangled Brush

desc:
Sheltered from the wind by the sharp ridge that rises up to the west and loops
round to the south, a tangled mass of wiry bushes has sprung up into a large
thicket. Tiny, arrow-shaped leaves cover the thorny branches and serve as a
filter from the harsh elements of the plains. The bushes grow thick and dense,
effectively preventing any passage to the north, but they appear to thin out
noticeably to the east.
name: Thicket

desc:
There grows short grass and a few bushes here and there in the field. A few,
lonely surviving tree have found their foothold here, but they look like dwarven
versions of their normal counterparts probably due to the arid soil. An old
ruins reside somewhere to the west, but apparently some people have made it a
home themselves. The ground is trampled from the heavy use, and forms a clear
path to the west.
name: Trampled Fields

desc:
The entrance of the camp has no gate, but there is a fence surrounding the area.
The ground is heavily trampled and crushed by frequent use, it's likely that the
inhabitants may have horses or some other animals. The ground has only a few
turfs of grass, and is otherwise dry and without any plant life. Some stones and
rocks have been piled at the root of the fence to make it sturdier. The camp
resides to the west.
name: At the Entrance to a Camp

desc:
This is the pathway to a camp that someone built next to the ancient broken
road. Several tents has been set up in the camp, and a quickly created, but
still sturdy fence surrounds the area. A tent has been placed to the south of
here. In the north, it looks like there are some fields.
name: Trail next to a Tent

desc:
This is about middle of a camp, a rectangular tent has been set up to the north,
and a large, more circular tent to the west. Some smoke rises up from most of
the tents, and a big bonfire has been built here. To the south, a quickly built
stable resides amongst the rubble of a building.
name: Middle of a Camp

desc:
This is a muddy area on the yard, there is a depression that stretches to the
east. The bottom of the depression is muddy, apparently some water accumulates
there. There are lots of animal prints on the muddy ground, a sort of trail has
formed there that stretches to the south.
name: Miserable Yard

desc:
A large, circular tent has been set up to the west. The ground is trampled by a
frequent use. A few more tents have been set up in the surroundings. Some old
ruins reside in the south parts of the camp area. A fence has been built
surrounding the camp.
name: Beside a Large Tent

desc:
A well has been dug here, in a slight depression of a trampled field. A circle
of mortared stones around the well is topped by a wooden bar. A wooden bar, long
rope, and a worn wooden bucket can be used for drawing water from the well.
Beyond a fence to the west, there are more fields that seem to continue
endlessly. The ruins of a small building are visible to the south.
name: At a Well in a Camp

desc:
This is old and collapsed part of a building, but it is serving now as a stable
for animals. The roof has been fixed, there are pinewood logs stretched between
the walls and the gaps have been sealed with tar. The old stone walls were
partly also collapsed, but they have also been fixed with pinewood planks.
name: Old and Collapsed Ruins

desc:
This small tent was made of coarse canvas, and has a flap functioning as a door.
Some stones surround the small fireplace that is in the middle of the tent. Furs
and mattresses lie around the tent working as sleeping places. The tent was set
up using poles, that support the tightly stretched canvas.
name: Rough Tent

desc:
This is a heavily trampled area, due to the pasture area to the north. Some of
the grain grows here wildly, among the weeds and other plants. The fence is
bordering the path, preventing animals and people travelling freely. Only a
couple of stunted pine trees grow in the area.
name: Path to Some Pastures

desc:
This tent is long and rectangular. There are several wooden poles in the middle
of the tent set up in a straight line. There are sleeping berths on the both
sides of the poles. Two fireplaces are in this long tent, placed symmetrically
on the both sides of the middle of the tent.
name: Rectangular Tent

desc:
This pasture is still growing grain and long grass, and apparently somewhat
taken care of. The ground has markings of animal hooves on the ground, probably
from domestic animals. The fence is encircling the area, keeping the animals in
and predators out. There are a few boulders on the side of the area, maybe
cleaned away from the field.
name: Seedy Pastures

desc:
This pasture is in relatively good shape, it has a couple of apple trees growing
in the corner of the area. Some grain and long grass grow wildly in the pasture,
providing some food for keeping animals fed. A few pine trees grow next to the
fence, and a few wild bushes with some berries.
name: Seedy Pastures

desc:
This is a large tent made of coarse canvas and hard leather. There is a small
fireplace in the middle of the tent and in the roof there is a hole for the
smoke to escape. Some wood have been piled up nearby the fireplace. A pungent
odour of wood burning is apparent in the tent. On the floor, there are some
rugs, mats, and straws of hay. A bed made of hay and leathers and furs stands
against one wall of the tent.
name: Large Tent

desc:
Lush green grass and brightly coloured wildflowers carpet this vast, level
meadowland. The air is filled with the scent of warm grass and rich topsoil and
the low buzzing of small bumblebees can be heard as they fly from one open
flower to the next. A small stream can be seen running on its course through the
plains the south and east, and a road is barely visible to the north and west.
name: In a Meadow

desc:
This field of grasses and shrubs lies near an old road which meets a stream to
the south. There is a small hill to the east that is covered in rocks and
various types of grasses. To the north a large field of boulders and shrubs can
be seen.
name: Grassy Field near a Road

desc:
The field here is broken and roughened by the upheaving of the land. The lack of
moisture in this part of the grassland has had a severe impact on what can grow
and what can not. There is little of note other than broken clods of dirt and
low tufts of grass. To the north and west an ancient road winds around this
field, while an upright slope of a rocky hill rises to the east.
name: Rough Fields

desc:
These hills are broken and bare. The frost damage done here to the soil has
caused it to heave and crack. The ground is barren, and it looks to have been
this way for a long time. The hills continue to the north, while to the east
large rocks can be seen below the hills.
name: Rough Hills

desc:
The rickety old cabin is filled with dust and cobwebs. The interior is very
shabby and it seems the cabin is soon to collapse. In the middle of the room,
which seems to serve as both a living room and bedroom, stands a small table.
The bed is nothing more than a pallet in the corner and a wooden structure on
the wall serves as a closet.
name: Dusty Old Cabin

desc:
The claw marks on the ground indicate that a huge animal built this burrow
through the dirt. The walls and floor are constructed of dark soil that is laced
with varying-diameter tree roots and the occasional stones. The natural tunnel's
exit is hidden by a thick foliage.
name: Wolf Den

desc:
A scattering of sunlight reaches partway into this cave, giving food to small
patches of red and green moss. The dirt floor is covered in footprints of
various sizes, both large and small. The larger footprints sink deep into the
hard earth, as if a heavy beast created them. Tiny, pointed pebbles, and dead
grass have collected in the large depressions, their meandering paths across the
grassy plains outside the caves coming to an end here.
name: Mossy Cavern

desc:
Large, bulbous boulders form the base of this large cave, and their large, beady
faces form a deeply uneven surface on which to walk. No natural light reaches
this far into the cavern, and the only signs of plant life are the withering
tree roots that poke through the walls of dirt. A jumbled mound of dirt, and
rocks of various sizes sits at the base of the western wall, perhaps a sign of
digging, or excavation.
name: Bumpy Grotto

desc:
Clumps of dead grass, and a scattering of dirt, pebbles, and feathers have
collected in this shallow inlet that has formed within the hills. Serrated rocks
poke through the dirt floor, creating a tripping hazard for unwary travellers,
and a potential death trap for those who trip.
name: Dusty Inlet

desc:
A trickle of water seeps from a crack in the rocky cave wall, covering the brown
floor in a layer of mud and slippery rocks. Large boulders embedded within the
walls create permanent, deep shadows on the soupy floor, and provide excellent
cover for small animals and insects.
name: Watery Brown Cave

desc:
The brown walls of this cave are speckled with small sections of white quartz,
and the dying ends of furry tree roots. The ground is a slippery mix of mud and
sheer rock, and a jumbled mix of large and small footprints are imprinted into
its surface. A small hole near the western wall is filled with a dark liquid of
unknown origin.
name: Damp Brown Cave

desc:
This grass covered hill drops quickly to the north and east. The absence of any
tall vegetation allows for excellent visibility in all directions for several
miles. Running along the northern margin of the hill, a small stream cuts
through the countryside. To the west, an old and decaying road makes its way
from north to south.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
Low grass grows here between large boulders, and the plains are littered with
small, thorny bushes of all types which seem to grow more dense to the south. To
the north some hills dot the horizon and to the west an old road marches on.
name: Brush-strewn Plains

desc:
Sheltered against the tall hills to the east, some dense foliage has flourished
here making a soft rustle with each movement. Bristly shrubs, cracked by lack of
water, crunch and writhe at the slightest wind. Southwards seems impassable
while thick arid undergrowth dominates the west.
name: Crackling Brush

desc:
Dry grass and shrubs grow out of the countless cracks in the hard, dry clay
here. With each puff, a wind causes a constant crackling in the foliage. To the
east the foliage continues. North, west, and south, an ancient road can be spied
through the bushes, meandering along.
name: Crackling Brush

desc:
A general lack of vegetation can be found leading down into this dark wolf lair
hinting at its activity. The lair is fairly clean, but carcass parts including
bones, antlers, and hair have been brought by the pack's members and have been
strewn about. The only way out of the lair is to go up.
name: Wolf Lair

desc:
To the east, brush-covered plains sprawl out, and on the horizon the outline of
distant mountains kisses the sky. To the north, nature and the hand of man fight
for dominance of the land, where the plains meet an old, ruined roadway. Thick
brush, short tussocks of coarse grass, and scattered rocks litter the landscape
here. A rocky shelf that stands above the plain to the west and south must be
the origin for the many boulders that have come to rest here.
name: Windy Brushlands

desc:
The plains stretch out in all directions, overgrown in this area by an abundance
of low, thorny bushes. Their almost-naked branches have become twisted and
tangled by the cruel prevailing winds. The ground is rather parched here, and
the only other vegetation to be seen in the thin, rocky soil are a few stunted
weeds and patches of coarse, yellowing grasses. Small rocks and larger boulders
lie all about.
name: Bristly Brush

desc:
Thick brush nestles here, thriving in a quite sheltered area, at least compared
to the more open plains hereabouts. A rocky shelf stands to the south and west,
forcing the chill winds that assail these lands upward and away from the ground.
The result is a thicker layer of topsoil that can better support the vegetation.
name: Brushy Plains

desc:
The small trail takes a sharp turn here bordering a rocky promontory that boasts
a commanding view of the plains stretching out below. Little vegetation is to be
seen on this stony table, which is so exposed to the blustery winds howling
throughout these lands, that no topsoil worth speaking of remains. Still, a few
tiny flowers sprout from cracks in the rock. Many, however, have been crushed
underfoot, just one sign that this area is used as a camp.
name: Small Camp on a Plateau

desc:
The trail bends to the east and to the north here, running for a short time
along the foot of a high cliff, climbing steadily as it does so. The mountain
range to the south is tall and imposing, and it is not possible to scale its
heights from this location. The topsoil is thin, probably as a result of the
punishing winds that torment these lands month after month. No vegetation
thrives here except for a few small, flowers that peep up through cracks in the
rock, and there is little to mark the trail from the stony ground alongside it.
name: Rising Trail at the Foot of a High Cliff

desc:
Cutting across the edge of a rocky plateau, an old, and barely noticeable trail
makes its way from east to west. Running westward for quite some distance, it
comes to an abrupt end however, not far to the east of here, where it meets up
with a large rock pile. The massive wall of fallen rocks and boulders extends
far to the south with a narrow gap threading its way through.
name: Old Trail

desc:
The more exposed parts of the plateau that lie further north are bare and rocky,
but this small box canyon is fertile. It has become a meadow, protected on three
sides from the chill winds that whip across the rest of these lands, denuding
them of vegetation and topsoil.
name: Box Canyon in the Hollin Ridge

desc:
Rising steeply towards the peaks to the west, this mountainous slope faces the
plains that lie north of the spur. The descent to the north is sharp, ending in
a treacherous field of scree that marches down to the top of a tall cliff. East
the going becomes a little easier, as the slope lessens.
name: Slope on the North Face of a Spur

desc:
Green grass grows here in abundance, cascading over the cliff to the east.
Nestling in the protection of the mountains, and of a range of hills that stands
to the north, the valley stretches out to the east, a veritable oasis of
vegetation amid the poor plains that characterize this region.
name: Cliff Overlooking a Valley

desc:
Magnificent peaks stand atop unscalable slopes to the east and north of here.
The entrance to a cave opens up to the south, while to the west there seems to
be a faint path where small stones and rocks have been kicked out of the way.
name: Mountain Slope

desc:
Craggy slopes roll down to the east, allowing for a possible descent down the
mountainside. To the south, jagged cliffs plummet downwards making it impossible
to traverse. White flowers peek out from behind many of the grey rocks.
name: On a Mountain

desc:
Rolling mountains slope downwards to the east and south. The slope comes to an
abrupt end just to the north of here at the top of a high cliff. Though it
offers an unparalleled vista of the lands below, this cliff stands at such a
height above the plains that no real detail is visible from here.
name: High Cliff

desc:
A huge pit cuts through the floor of this cave. Stretching several yards in
length, it would be impossible to cross if it wasn't for the crude, rope bridge
stretched across it. Darkness rises up from the depths of the chasm, and at the
bottom, a void devours all knowledge of what could be known of that place.
name: Gaping Chasm

desc:
A stone arch, in defiance of nature and time, stands alone in the centre of the
cave. The walls are made of a blackish stone with veins of some silvery rock
branching up to the ceiling. Much of the dark stone that comprises the walls has
fallen to the ground creating a black shroud along the outer edges of the
cavern.
name: Dark Grotto

desc:
Great slabs of rock, protruding from the walls, stab their way into the centre
of this cave. Like knives piercing a skull, the rock slabs make it impossible to
simply walk straight through the cave, instead demanding a zigzag route. North,
east and west the walls close in, and the only way out lies south.
name: Greystone Chamber

desc:
A lush valley stretches to the north and west, its floor an ocean of tall, dark
green grasses that wave gently in the wind. Protected to the north by a range of
hills, and to the south by the rising mountains, this vale enjoys much milder
weather conditions than most of the nearby lands. A few scattered broadleaf
trees, and numerous low bushes thrive here, especially nearing the feet of the
cliffs to the south and east, where a dense hedge of thorny shrubs has come to
dominate the landscape.
name: Lush Valley by the Foot of a Cliff

desc:
A lush valley extends out in all directions save to the south, where it runs
right to the foot of a massive cliff. The mountain spur is only a low footnote
to the Misty Mountains that lie further east, but the cliffs nonetheless afford
this vale a good deal of protection from the harsh weather that blights much of
the rest of the land hereabouts. As a result, the topsoil is thicker and richer
here, supporting not only a thick sea of dark green grass, but also scattered
broadleaf trees, and any number of low shrubs. A dense hedge of thorny bushes
dominates the land at the very foot of the cliffs.
name: Lush Valley by the Foot of a Cliff

desc:
Extending for several miles to the north, east and west, this lush valley comes
to an abrupt halt here, its grassy floor marching right to the feet of a long
mountain spur. The cliffs loom high above, but the vegetation still thrives
here, despite the diminished access to sunlight. A pool of clear water has
collected here surrounded by flourishing greenery.
name: Watering Hole

desc:
The green valley continues to the east, marching along the foot of a towering
mountain range. The landscape is a sea of lush, green grass, growing in places
as tall as a man. The soil is deep, rich and loamy. With the hills that stand to
the north, and the mountain spur to the south, this land is well protected, and
in addition to the grasses, a number of scattered trees and many shrubs also
flourish here.
name: Lush Valley by the Foot of a Cliff

desc:
The valley is covered with lush, green grasses and bushes. From here, the green
hills to the north and purple mountains to the south seem far away, yet they
both loom over the valley. The soil seems fertile and would make good farmland
if it were settled.
name: Lush Valley

desc:
The valley extends for some miles both to the east and west, and reaches to the
foot of the mountains in the south. The deep, loamy soil evidently has an ample
supply of water, for lush, green grass covers the ground all around. A few
scattered trees and many bushes also dot the landscape. This secluded valley
would make excellent farmland if anyone were to settle here. But as it lies so
very far from civilisation this would seem an unlikely prospect.
name: Deep in a Lush Valley

desc:
A few groves of tall trees dot the landscape, utilizing the excellent ground and
ample moisture. The tall grass may hide wildlife, and there are trails worn in
the grass that indicate larger animals make their way around here. The hills to
the north can be reached from here following a sharp ascent and the mountains to
the south rise up some distance away.
name: Lush Valley Nearing the Hills

desc:
The valley is covered by tall grass here with some scattered green, broadleaf
trees growing here and there. Nestling in the shelter of a range of hills that
lies to the north, and a mountain spur to the south, these lands are quite well
protected from the fiercest weather, with the result that vegetation flourishes
throughout the vale.
name: Lush Valley Nearing the Hills

desc:
The rolling hills continue to the east, north and west of here, their grassy
slopes stretching off into the distance. Grass grows in abundance in and among
the many large boulders that have come to rest here. Down a gentle slope to the
south a lush valley opens up, covered by tall green grass. And further in the
distance, across the valley, a rocky spur of the Misty Mountains can be seen
towering above everything.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
In the south the many peaks of the Misty Mountains are visible. Their tall peaks
reaching to the clouds. A lush green valley lies before the mountains and these
rolling hills mark the northern boundary of that lush land. Tumbling down toward
the vale, the slope gradually becomes grassier, and the vegetation much greener.
name: Grassy hills

desc:
The hills empty into a lush valley to the south and west. A few scattered trees
grow here on the descending slopes of the hills, but not in any abundance. Far
across the valley, a tall mountainous spur cuts the land from east to west, and
it is this, together with the hills, that have created the rich vale that opens
out not far to the south.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
Bordered to the west by steep slopes, covered in thick, thorny brush, a lush
valley opens out all around. It tumbles down from the hillside to the north,
terminating only some miles at the feet of a mountain spur. Thick, dark green
grass carpets the entire area, with a few scattered trees and a number of
healthy shrubs also to be seen.
name: Lush Valley Nearing the Hills

desc:
The west slope of this valley ends with a combination of large boulders and
thick thorny brush, blocking further travel in that direction. Stretching out to
the east, and widening in the south, the vale is full of lush, green grass that
grows in many places as tall as a man. A few trees and bushes stand off in the
distance.
name: Lush Valley

desc:
From the west a steep, bushy slope quickly descends. The overgrown, thorny
thicket that covers its flanks seems to extend for miles both to the north and
to the south. Extending eastward is a lush vale where grasses of all types grow
in great abundance. Tucked away between a range of hills to the north and a tall
mountain spur to the south, this valley is provided with ample protection from
the elements, making it richer in vegetation than any area for many miles.
name: Lush Valley

desc:
The hills taper off to the east and south, revealing a wide valley between them
and the mountainous spur that lies further to the south. The hills run parallel
to this valley for a long distance east from here, their slopes covered with
medium-length, lush, green grass that merges smoothly with the taller grasses of
the vale below. The faintest scar of a road can be seen cutting across the land,
several miles to the west, but access to that region is blocked from here by an
extensive area of extremely thick brush.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
The waters of a cold, clear stream cut across the plains, flowing here from east
to north. The channel is rather wide at this point, and the water flows by so
lazily that it almost appears not to move at all. The southern bank can be
reached by means of some quick-footed action over some slippery looking
boulders.
name: Lazy Stream

desc:
The plains here are a collection of tall green grasses. It is obvious that the
stream feeds into here, as the ground has a slightly marshy feel to it. Water
collects in footprints left here. A stream flows past this field to the east and
north. To the south the ground becomes harder as it meets the slope of a hill.
To the west the plains continue until they meet hills.
name: Tall Grass

desc:
These hills descend rapidly to the north and east, while they rise steadily to
the south and west. The land here is a sea of soft, green grass that stretches
all the way down the hillside to meet what appear to be lush plains that lie to
the north.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
A small field can be seen over a slight rise here, while the landscape descends
to the north. The slopes continue to roll further in the other directions. This
hillside is covered with a scattering of small trees and low brush, that are
just enough to offer scant protection against the winds and rain, but not
sufficient to afford any real shelter from the elements.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
A few solitary pines stand amidst the medium sized grasses that cover the
hillsides here. The hills continue in all directions, rolling off well into the
distance. To the south and east, far beyond the hills, tall mountains rise into
the sky.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
Tall, slender evergreens grow in abundance here, yet never drawing so close to
one-another as to constitute a true forest. The ground alternates between a deep
litter of old pine needles close by the trees, and thick, green grasses that
bend in the wind howling between the trunks. An occasional stump stands amid the
grasses, covered with moss, and attesting to the age of this grove. The hills
continue far to the south while slowly descending in the west. To the north and
east the descent is more marked, as the hills give way to the plains that
stretch out below.
name: Lightly Wooded Hillside

desc:
The lands here are parched, despite their location between a lush stretch of
fields lying near a waterway to the north and east, and a band of grassy hills
crossing the land from southeast to northwest, which passes by not more than a
couple of miles from here. In this area, only a few low, tussocks of dull, dry,
yellow-brown grasses have managed to survive.
name: Arid Plains

desc:
The hills are covered with grasses, with the occasional fir or pine standing
alone on the slopes. The high ground continues to the east and west, before
descending to meet the plains below. To the north, the descent is sharp, but in
other directions the hills tail lazily off towards the lower lands. Far to the
south and to the east, mountain ranges stretch across the horizon.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
The hills offer a view of the plains to the south and west of here. Partially
hidden by thick brush, further to the west a road that has seen better days runs
north and south. Covered with grasses and not as dry as the plains below, more
hills roll to the north and east and quickly become covered with scattered
trees. A mountain range to the south looms over the dry prairies.
name: Grassy Hill

desc:
A few scattered pine trees grow upon the rising slope of the hills. They offer
only the most sparse of shade, or shelter from the prevailing winds. Between and
around the trees are many old stumps, which show distinct signs of rotting. The
hills continue to the south and west, with grasslands stretching out to the
east. To the north the ground seems to become less solid underfoot.
name: Scattered Pines

desc:
Soft, green grass carpets the hills, and thick bushes grow all about, offering a
wind break even at the very crests of the hills. An occasional boulder rests
amid the vegetation. These have obviously been here for quite some time, as
their northern faces have collected a thick coating of moss and lichen.
name: Green Hills

desc:
Rolling playfully, the hills dance around this area and connect with the green
hills that rise to the south. Here the land is characterised mostly by rock,
with but a thin covering of moss and other greenery growing in the cracks, while
a small creek can be heard gurgling to the north.
name: Riverside Hills

desc:
The stream makes a slow arc from the east and turns to head in a northerly
direction. The current is mild and the flow of the water smooth as it makes its
journey from the eastern mountains and on through the green plains. But though
the waters are leisurely, they have still undercut the western bank somewhat,
making it impossible to scale, despite its low height. Tall clusters of reeds
and grass thrive on the never-ending source of fresh water and a few fish can be
glimpsed darting below the surface of the stream.
name: Bend in the Stream

desc:
This narrow stream weaves and snakes through the fertile fields and plains. The
strength of the current is not powerful enough to cut a new path through the
countryside but instead the stream turns to follow the lay of the land when
necessary. A wide expanse of flat, green fields can be seen to the east.
name: Meandering Stream

desc:
Following a wide curve, the slow-moving waters change direction towards the
north where the stream meets up with a second tributary. The muddy banks of the
water are steep and slick here, making them quite impossible to scale. A road
follows the stream to the west and grass-covered plains lie to the south.
name: Bend in the Stream

desc:
Foliage snakes its way through down from hills to the west and explodes into
full bloom in this marshy riverbank. Surrounded by the hills to the west and
south and a brook to the north, the greenery here peacefully grows in the
shallows, oblivious to the hardships of the world.
name: Marshy Riverside

desc:
The plains are dry here, despite a stream that cuts across the land to the
north. The thin topsoil that rests atop a rocky substrate is able to retain so
little moisture that many large cracks have appeared all around. Small patches
of brown grass barely hold onto life in this arid area, growing with difficulty
between the cracks. Further away in all directions the land becomes more
fertile.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
The grass is thick and slick underfoot but caked in mud. Water squelches out of
the ground at each step. To the south and west green hills can be reached,
rising at first with a barely perceptible ascent from the plains. A small stream
can be seen at some distance to the northeast.
name: Muddy Fields

desc:
The soil here is more mud than anything else, and the grasses are slick with
dew. From the north and east comes the distinct sound of a babbling brook. Far
to the south the plains start to rise gently towards a range of green hills that
climbs from the horizon.
name: Mixed Ground

desc:
The water of this stream runs clear and very cold, having originated high on the
mountains. It is not too deep, and its stone bed can clearly be seen, its shape
dancing with the refraction of the water. Both banks are covered with thick,
green grass interrupted only occasionally by an isolated large rock. Small
minnow schools can be seen flitting about.
name: Glittering Stream

desc:
This part of the stream at first glance appears quite deep, but upon closer
inspection the bottom silt can be made out quite clearly. The banks can be seen
in the distance. The water is very cold and clear as it runs by swiftly. The
occasional school of minnows flits by and larger darker shapes can be discerned
which can only be larger fish. Plains can be seen to the east and west, and
beyond them hills which eventually join a large mountain range to the south.
name: Shallow Crossing

desc:
The grasses turn a boggy carpet to the north and east where they draw closer to
a stream, and diminish in health to the south. The landscape is remarkably flat
in this region, though some miles to the south a range of hills rises up from
the plains. To the east, the Misty Mountains dominate the horizon.
name: Marshy Ground

desc:
The water is crystal clear here with several large boulders providing some deep,
and almost still pools near to either bank. Flowing from the south, the waters
turn westward here, but the current is too languid to undercut the bank too
much. Far to the south, beyond the dry plains, hills join a rocky mountain
range.
name: Quiet Stream

desc:
The stream continues to flow slowly here, turning now from its northerly course
to travel due west. The ripple of water against the few large stones does not
make much sound and the surface of the stream remains, for the most part, as
smooth as glass. A few small fish can briefly be spied, darting about below the
surface of the water. Upstream, to the south, the water widens noticeably, but
at the same time becomes more shallow. Vast fields roll on beyond the steep
river banks, seemingly forever, to both the north and the east.
name: Along a Lazy Waterway

desc:
Green fields of knee-high grass spread out all around as far as the eye can see.
A gentle stream that flows by to the south and west is the only visible feature
anywhere at all close by in this flat country. There are little or no sounds, or
visible evidence of wildlife. Rising up, far in the southeast, the faint, yet
distinct shape of a mighty mountain range reaches towards the sky.
name: Field by a Stream

desc:
These green hills descend quickly to the north and south, making passage not
viable in those directions. The stream to the south provides enough moisture to
sustain the healthy mixture of tall grass and wild grains that flourishes across
this meadowland. The brown heads of wild grains nod gently with each breath of
wind, and small animals are barely glimpsed as they use the thick vegetation as
cover from their predators.
name: Hills by a Stream

desc:
The grasses and small brush merge together making this area very green. There is
a slight elevation to the land which gives the appearance of several small
hills. These gently rolling lands stop abruptly to the east at a crevice, and to
the west some green hills loom in the distance. A taller variety of grass
becomes dominant to the south, perhaps fed by the nearby stream.
name: Rolling Grasslands

desc:
The ground cover in these fields is thick and green and the tall stalks of grass
wave gently with the occasional breezes that gust across the lands. The thick
vegetation and close proximity of fresh, running water must make this an ideal
environment for all manner of small game. In the distance the outline of the
Misty Mountains looms high on the horizon.
name: Fertile Fields

desc:
Blanketed by an abundance of short grass that covers every inch of ground, and
bordered to the south by a small stream, these hills would make an excellent
grazing area for wild animals. The lack of tall vegetation or trees provides a
clear view of the surrounding fields and plains that roll out to the east, west,
and north.
name: Green Hills

desc:
A healthy mix of grass and wildflowers blankets the hills. Clover, in
particular, grows especially well, flourishing in the rich, fertile soil and
filling the air with its heavy scent. The hills continue to the east, and to the
north, lie some fertile fields in the lower ground. A stream flows through the
fields in the south, but the steep bank does not allow a safe descend.
name: Green Hills

desc:
A golden crop of wild wheat thrives in the rich soil, the brown heads of grain
waving gently back and forth at the slightest breath of air. There are many
suitable holes and hiding places for small animals in the tall stalks of grain
to run. The fertile fields continue to the west and a small stream runs directly
north of the fields. The bank is too steep, with rocky shore to descend safely.
name: Fertile Fields

desc:
Provided with sufficient moisture by the streams that border these lush fields,
the vegetation grows thick and plentiful in the dark, rich soil. Tall grass and
weeds flourish together in abundance, supplying excellent cover for the various
small plains animals that make their home in this area. The green fields roll on
to the east.
name: Lush Fields

desc:
Tough grasses grow low to the ground among these gently rolling plains that
stretch out into the distance in all directions. To the west, however, the
vegetation starts to change in character, as these wild grasses start to give
way to patches of a more cultivated cereal that suggest that at one time those
lands were perhaps cultivated.
name: Rolling Fields

desc:
Tall, green grasses stand close together as these hills are fed by a nearby
stream. The stalks are tall enough to provide cover to small game. Wind that
flows through here can be heard as the grass rustles in response to it. The
fields stretch to the south, and hills to the east, but they dry out
considerably. A stream winds its way around the field to the north and west.
name: Verdant Hills

desc:
On the hills, bushes and small, dry-looking trees spring up between dry grasses
here. There are small hills of sandy looking soil that are mounded up as well,
giving the area a very dry appearance. To the east, the hills continue while
there is fresh looking grass to the west.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
The hills here grow some bushes and small trees that become increasingly greener
the further east it extends. From a marshy area to the east a smell of stale
water emerges. There are grasslands to the south, and to the north a small
stream can be seen. The hills continue off to the west.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
The fields continue here with short, green grasses. The grass grows low to the
ground and can hide only small animals. The wind blows through the grass and a
rippling among the field can be seen as well as heard. The fields continue on to
the east. In the south, an impassable thicket of brushes block the way.
name: Grassy Fields

desc:
In the middle of a wide fields, there is a sharp crevice with steep walls to
other directions than to the south. The soil is dry and mostly gravely, but
there are some grasses and raspberries and other weed growing here and there.
The ground ascends to the south, where are green fields with more suitable
conditions for grain and other plants to survive.
name: Crevice in Some Fields

desc:
The fields here are not on level ground. The grasses that cover this area are
smaller in variety and completely cover the grounds. To the south there is a
sound of water faintly trickling as the fields meet a creek. There is a crevice
on the ground to the west, which prevent travelling in that direction. A field
of taller grass lies to the north.
name: Rolling Grasslands

desc:
The fields here are lush and green, fed by nearby streams. The moisture in the
soil continues to the north where a smell of stale water pervades. Deep and
muddy marsh ponds block the way to enter the marshes. To the east and west the
grassy fields continue. There are some rolling small hills to the south.
name: Grassy Fields

desc:
Small fish dart beneath the surface of the clear water of this stream, flashing
silver as their thin bodies catch and reflect any available light. The bed of
the stream is covered with small pebbles, made smooth by the continuous flow of
water, and by a few larger rocks that provide a hiding place for the occasional
crayfish.
name: Gentle Stream

desc:
Travelling from east to west, the gentle stream flows on through the fields and
plains. The banks of the stream are cut high and sharp, suggesting that the
water level rises substantially during the more rainy seasons. The land to the
south looks wet and swampy. Far to the south, the hazy purple outline of the
Misty Mountains looms above the horizon.
name: Gentle Stream

desc:
With a loud splash of rushing water, the streams from the north and the south
meet here to form one body of water which flows on to the west. Although the
current is quick and the expanse of the water is wide, the stream is actually
deceptively shallow. Grass covered plains stretch out to the east but the river
bank is too steep to allow access in that direction.
name: Confluence

desc:
Arising to the east as two smaller streams, this minor tributary to the Bruinen
is nevertheless wide at this point, as it runs fast and shallow through the
plains. Full of dirt and silt picked up from its travels in the countryside, the
muddy body of water continues toward its western destination. A little way to
the west a ford is visible, spanning the distance between two parts of an old
and decaying roadway that travels north and south.
name: Tributary to Bruinen

desc:
The meandering stream shifts from its western course and begins gradually to
turn south, heading towards what appears to be a merging point with another,
smaller tributary. Dirt and silt collected by the stream during its journey
through the plains have muddied the waters making it impossible to see the
stream bed. The banks of the stream to the north and west are extremely low and
it is likely that the surrounding land would be flooded during the rainy season,
to spill waters over the road.
name: Bend in the Stream

desc:
The shallow stream trips and falls over rocks and stones as it continues on its
twisted course in the plains. Various water plants stretch their thirsty roots
deep into the stream, and tall reeds flourish along the banks of the water,
nodding and swaying gently with each soft gust of wind that sweeps out from over
the plains. A large, green meadow lies directly to the north.
name: Muddy Stream

desc:
Twisting lazily from the north and turning to the west, the stream meanders on
its way through the land. The banks of the water are high, making the fields and
hills to the east and south easily accessible. Hidden within the reeds bordering
the very edge of the water are small nests that look as though they were built
by waterfowl. The marshy ground appears to be a prime breeding ground for small
insects.
name: Languid Waterway

desc:
The elevated bank to the west forces the stream to change direction and turn
from the east to the south. The water is thick with silt and topsoil from the
plains, turning the once clear water a muddy brown, and the current sluggish.
The bank to the north is too steep to ascend, but it's possible to go on with
the stream to the south as it flows on its way toward the distant Bruinen, or to
the east towards the mountains.
name: Bend in the Stream

desc:
The stream runs swiftly and quietly at this point, heading west through the
prairie lands and eventually leading to the Bruinen itself. There is a distant
roar to the east that suggests the water becomes turbulent in that direction but
in this area the surface of the water remains unbroken by rocks or other
obstructions. The current moves too quickly to sustain any permanent plant life
other than to feed the roots of the odd tree which has managed to take hold on
the banks of the stream. A steep basin can be seen to the north and on the south
side of the river a hilly bank is apparent.
name: The Loseithel

desc:
The current of this small stream begins to quicken as it cascades over a series
of low waterfalls on its journey from east to west. The rushing water churns and
froths over the falls, sending up tiny clouds of mist at the bottom of each
fall. A sudden incline rises to the north, and beyond the hills in the high
southern bank nothing else is visible.
name: Turbulent Cascade

desc:
There is little in the way of vegetation in this area but the occasional tree
can be found having taken hold on the banks of the stream. The roots of the
trees have dug down and across, reaching out from the walls of the banks to dip
into the fresh running water below. Piles of debris and soil gathered by the
flow of the water through the plains have become tightly packed against the
tangled mess of the outstretched roots. The water itself has begun to grow muddy
and dirty from its contact with the topsoil of the land. As it travels past this
near blockage, the water gathers speed, as it heads towards a series of small
falls to the west.
name: The Loseithel

desc:
The quiet stream makes its way westward through the hills and plains. Meltwater
that originated as snow in the distant mountains, the water is still chilly and
so clear that the rock-covered bottom of the streambed can be seen perfectly. An
expansive grassland stretches out to the south and hills are visible directly to
the north.
name: The Loseithel

desc:
The low bank of the stream to the north appears to flood these lowlands on a
frequent basis as the ground is thick with mud, and water is standing in several
places. The musty smell of stagnant water hangs heavily in the air and there is
a faint hum as tiny insects swarm over the pools.
name: Marshy Lowlands

desc:
East and west the hills continue, while to the south a wide stream cuts across
the countryside. The river side of the hills appear to be flooded with some
frequency, the floodwaters arising from the stream. Tall clumps of grass,
interspersed with reeds, grow in the muddy earth. To the west the rear of a
run-down cottage can be seen.
name: Muddy Hills by a River

desc:
There is a good view of the lands to the south of here. The river flows joyfully
down below the hill, the descent to the river is very steep making the climb not
possible. There are some rocks jutting out of the hills, and there grow only
some small brushes and a few pine and ash trees.
name: Hills by a River

desc:
This small hill gives a view of the nearby lands. It is covered in boulders and
grasses, and could perhaps have been used as a lookout post at some time in the
past. There is a small cottage that stands close by the base of this hill, and
to the south a small stream tumbles past. A road runs north and south a little
way to the west of here.
name: Short Incline

desc:
This field is nearly covered by large boulders and gravel. Little of the once
fertile grasses that have grown here can be seen now. The dust that is kicked up
by the wind scatters the small pebbles that lie here, giving this field a barren
appearance. Grasses have sprung up to the east and south, whilst to the west
hills, similar in appearance to this field, can be seen. The field of rocks
continues north.
name: Rocky Fields

desc:
Short, green grasses grow in abundance here on the flat landscape. Some boulders
and other rocks have come to rest here, and the grasses and moss have almost
overtaken all but a few. The flat grassland continues to the east and south.
More rocks, larger than those here, have come to a rest to the north and west.
name: Flatlands

desc:
This field is covered in small stones and pebbles. A landslide probably moved
the covering soil away and left the rocks here. Small bunches of brown grass
spring up between the rocks, however the field is far too filed with stones to
be of any agricultural use. The fields continue in all directions with little
discernible change.
name: Rocky Fields

desc:
The soil is dry and cracked in this place. No moisture reaches here and the dust
is evident. The few scraggly weeds that have grown up from the cracks are little
more than yellowed, dead husks. A single, stunted tree once grew here, but that
has fallen to the ground, cracked and dry as a bone.
name: Dry Wasteland

desc:
The hills here are covered with rocks of every size. The brush and grasses that
squeeze between the stones give this area a sparse appearance. There are rocky
fields to the east and south of here, while there is a bit more vegetation to
the west and little growth to the north.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
The hills are broken up as the soil has been dug up all around here and left
uneven. Grasses and small bushes have continued to grow and cover the broken
land. A road can be seen under the ridge, running from the north around to the
west as it meanders its way through the fields. To the south and east the fields
continue.
name: Rough Hills

desc:
Someone or something has dug a huge pit in the hillside, it does not look like
it was naturally formed. Roots of some plants are poking through the ceiling and
the walls. On the ground, there are a lot of loose sand and stones, and some
bones and sticks and dry hay. The smell is quite strong here in this dusty and
stale pit.
name: Stale Pit

desc:
It would be a tremendous feat of nature for any life form to survive prolonged
exposure to the harsh elements in this area. The dusty hills are dry and the
ground is cracked in several places. There are no trees or shrubs to provide
shelter nor is there any water source visible. The hills descend slightly to the
east and continue to the north and south.
name: Barren Hills

desc:
Dry and lifeless, the slightest breath of wind kicks up whirlwinds of dust
across this gritty promontory. The soil lacks any nutrients and is filled with
cracks. An overgrown road lies directly to the west while the fields continue to
the south. Far off in the east the horizon is filled with high jagged peaks
creating the image of a large jaw jutting up from the earth.
name: Lifeless Promontory

desc:
Thin cracks spread out tenuously where the earth has split from the lack of
moisture, and small rocks litter the ground across these desolate fields. The
fields stretch out flat and lifeless to the east, while a steep ridge ascends to
the west and south. An old road is visible to the north.
name: Barren Fields

desc:
A distinct trail can be seen leading towards what appears to be a dilapidated
old farm to the south. Despite the fact that the property appears to be quite
large, the farm's inhabitants long ago abandoned it and allowed it to collapse
gradually. The trail ends at a high wooden fence enclosing the farm, with a
wooden gate blocking the entrance.
name: Gates of a Ruined Farm

desc:
To the south is an old structure with a collapsed straw roof, and a chimney that
protrudes into the sky. Grass and ivy are gradually engulfing its crumbling
walls. A big, abandoned barn made of medium-sized timbers is visible to the west
and a small crudely constructed stable is to the east.
name: Ruined Farm

desc:
With just a hitching post and a wooden roof to shield the animals from the
elements, the stable in this area is fairly spartanly built. The weight of the
animals has caused the clay floor to become harder. The only exit is to the
west, leading back toward the gates.
name: Stables

desc:
As a result of time and weather, the walls now clearly show the decaying
processes. The barn gate swings back and forth with wind gusts. Although the
barn door is securely closed by a sturdy chain wrapped around the gate's frame
and an iron lock.
name: Ruined Barn

desc:
The old farm ruin looks to have deteriorated gradually over time. The building's
straw roof portion fell in a little corner, leaving that area exposed to the
elements, but its stone walls are still standing. A section of the room with a
few straw-pellets appear to be utilized as a sleeping place, and the furniture
appears to have been used recently.
name: Ruined Farmhouse

desc:
Ancient boulders rest upon an area of cracked, rocky ground that has almost no
visible topsoil to sustain life. And yet among the rocks grows a proliferation
of low bushes, their naked branches tangled and contorted by the prevailing
winds. Dry, yellow grasses also to sprout from many cracks in the ground. To the
west, the last remains of an ancient roadway cut across the land.
name: Broken Plains

desc:
The plains rise gradually to the south as they start their long approach toward
a distant mountain spur. The ground underfoot is parched and cracked. A scant
covering of yellow-brown grasses pokes up here and there, clinging feebly to
life. A thick hedge has growth to the south making travel in that direction
difficult.
name: Broken Plains

desc:
This is an area of transition where broken ground, with only a thin covering of
yellow-brown grasses, gives way to dense thickets of dry, twisted shrubs. Though
plainly struggling to hang on to life in this harsh environment, the bushes
nevertheless constitute a dense thicket that nestles in the shelter at the foot
of the hills to the east, and blocks travel up their slopes.
name: Thick Brush

desc:
These are old ruins that have recently been rebuilt to have a new roof and fixed
walls. While the walls collapsed long ago, the western wall appears to have been
freshly built and a small plaque has been affixed to it. Pinewood logs were used
to fix the wall, the other walls are still mostly in the original condition.
However, nearest to the roof, there were some holes in the other walls that had
to be filled with wood. The roof is completely new. It was made of wooden planks
and sealed with tar.
name: Old Ruins

desc:
This old cellar is robustly made and have survived the test of time for what
might be centuries. The floor is made of flat stones, evidently carefully picked
to fit neatly together. The stone stairs lead upwards out of the cellar.
name: Old Cellar

desc:
The old cellar has a musty, stale scent. The stone walls have experienced the
ravages of time, and frost damage has caused numerous noticeable fractures,
allowing gnarly roots and vines to take up residence in the cellar. A messy
layer of muck, grime, and water puddles has been spread over the formerly
spotless paved stone surface. Thick oaken beams support a ceiling constructed of
half-rotten planks that have collapsed in some places and deposited muck on the
floor.
name: Wine Cellar

desc:
This hidden pit lies deep underneath a fallen tree, the home of a termite
colony. A multitude of tiny holes can be seen extending in all directions where
the workers have dug to expand the nest. This huge pit is where larger workers
reside that are unable to fit into the smaller tunnels.
name: Hidden Pit

desc:
This foul cave seems to be the home of a predator. The walls are made of hard
rock and extremely rough as if something was digging around. Several patches of
black fur cover the ground, as well as piles of bones and half-eaten corpses in
various states of decay which emanate a penetrating odour. A deep footprint can
be seen near the entrance, making it obvious the inhabitant of this cave must be
both large and heavy.
name: Foul Cave

desc:
Someone has recently built a covered lookout on the top of the building. The
walls are made of decent quality planks, and look sturdy. There are window-like
openings in the walls, but no glass. The wind breezes through and it's easy to
observe anything that is happening in the nearby surroundings. The ancient
broken road is visible to the east and south.
name: Roof of a Ruined Building
